talk_title: Phase 2 neoadjuvant trastuzumab tucatinib plus chemotherapy in HER2‑amplified locally advanced rectal cancer

speaker.name: Mike Foote
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, GI Oncology

targets.dependency_context: HER2‑amplified KRAS wild‑type locally advanced rectal cancer
HER2‑amplified KRAS wild‑type locally advanced rectal cancer

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC 3+
biomarkers.finding: All patients achieving clinical complete response had HER2 IHC 3+ (100% vs 10% in non‑responders)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance
biomarkers.finding: Patients with ctDNA clearance after neoadjuvant therapy had higher cCR rates (70% vs 0%)

models.name: Phase 2 HER2‑targeted neoadjuvant rectal cancer trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑amplified, KRAS wild‑type, MMR proficient, locally advanced rectal adenocarcinoma (n=10 enrolled, 9 evaluable)

clinical_data.metric: overall response rate (ORR) after HER2‑only lead‑in

MOA_summary: Dual HER2 blockade with trastuzumab (antibody) and tucatinib (small‑molecule kinase inhibitor) inhibits HER2‑driven signaling in HER2‑amplified KRAS‑wild‑type rectal tumors, leading to high response rates and a 50% clinical complete response, enabling organ preservation in a subset of patients.
key_findings: HER2‑amplified locally advanced rectal cancer patients achieved a 78% ORR during a 6‑week HER2‑only lead‑in.
50% of patients attained a clinical complete response after combined HER2 blockade and chemotherapy, allowing non‑operative management.
HER2 IHC 3+ staining predicted clinical complete response, whereas lower IHC scores correlated with residual disease.
ctDNA dynamics tracked response and early recurrence; persistent ctDNA after neoadjuvant therapy identified patients at high risk of relapse.
No grade ≥3 toxicities were observed, confirming safety of the HER2‑targeted neoadjuvant regimen.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with KRAS mutations were excluded as they confer resistance to HER2‑targeted therapy.
Three patients with initial cCR later recurred locally, all retaining HER2 positivity, suggesting possible need for prolonged HER2 inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Add cytotoxic backbone to enhance tumor shrinkage and eradicate HER2‑negative clones.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased cCR rate to 50% versus historical 20% with chemo alone

combination_strategies.rationale: Consolidate local control for patients with residual disease after HER2‑targeted therapy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved local control; 63% organ preservation at 2.5 years

open_questions: Can HER2‑targeted neoadjuvant therapy replace chemotherapy entirely in HER2‑amplified rectal cancer?
What is the optimal duration of HER2 blockade to prevent late local recurrences?
Would other HER2 agents (e.g., ADCs) further improve cCR and organ preservation rates?

talk_title: Phase 2 neoadjuvant zenodatumab in early‑stage HER2‑positive breast cancer (NEO3NHER)

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC 3+
biomarkers.finding: All patients achieving pCR were HER2 IHC 3+ (100% vs 0% in IHC 2+)

biomarkers.name: ERBB2 amplification
biomarkers.finding: All four pCR patients harbored ERBB2 amplification

models.name: NEO3NHER phase 2 neoadjuvant study
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑positive (IHC 3+ or 2+ with amplification), ER‑positive 80%, n=20

MOA_summary: Zenodatumab is a biparatopic HER2 antibody that simultaneously engages the trastuzumab and pertuzumab epitopes, enhancing HER2 clustering, blocking downstream signaling, and mediating ADCC, resulting in a 30% pCR rate in HER2‑positive early breast cancer without chemotherapy.
key_findings: Zenodatumab monotherapy achieved a 30% pCR rate in HER2‑positive early breast cancer.
An additional 20% of patients achieved RCB‑1, indicating near‑complete responses.
All pCRs occurred in HER2 IHC 3+ tumors; HER2 IHC 2+ patients did not achieve pCR.
No grade ≥3 treatment‑related adverse events were observed, confirming a favorable safety profile.
Higher HER2 RNA expression and PAM50 HER2‑enriched subtype correlated with response, though not statistically significant.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with HER2 IHC 2+ showed lower response rates, suggesting a threshold effect for zenodatumab efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care for ER‑positive HER2‑positive disease; may synergize with HER2 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Allowed per investigator discretion; no safety signal observed

open_questions: Will extending zenodatumab duration beyond 5 months increase pCR rates?
Can zenodatumab replace chemotherapy in node‑positive HER2‑positive disease?
What is the predictive value of HER2‑DX or other quantitative HER2 assays for patient selection?

talk_title: Phase III perioperative atezolizumab plus chemotherapy in resectable stage II/III NSCLC (interim EFS and MRD analysis)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance (pre‑surgery)
biomarkers.finding: 75% clearance in atezolizumab arm vs 46% in placebo; clearance associated with higher PCR (70% vs 18%)

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Stratification factor; not directly linked to efficacy in interim analysis

models.name: Phase III atezolizumab + chemo vs placebo + chemo
models.genotype_or_key_features: Stage II/III NSCLC, EGFR/ALK wild‑type, PD‑L1 assessed, n=501

MOA_summary: Atezolizumab blocks PD‑L1, restoring anti‑tumor T‑cell activity when combined with standard chemotherapy in the peri‑operative setting, leading to improved EFS, higher MPR/pCR rates, and ctDNA clearance that predicts favorable outcomes.
key_findings: Atezolizumab plus chemotherapy improved 2‑year EFS to 74.8% versus 55.5% with placebo (HR 0.45).
MPR increased to 53.8% and pCR to 31.1% in the atezolizumab arm, far exceeding placebo.
Pre‑surgery ctDNA clearance occurred in 75% of atezolizumab patients versus 46% of placebo and correlated with higher PCR rates.
Grade ≥3 immune‑related adverse events were low (4.4%) and manageable.
ctDNA clearance was prognostic for EFS regardless of treatment arm.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with baseline ctDNA positivity who failed to clear ctDNA had markedly lower PCR and higher MRD rates.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy provides cytoreduction and may increase neoantigen release, synergizing with PD‑L1 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved EFS, MPR, and pCR compared with chemo alone

open_questions: Will ctDNA‑guided adjuvant therapy escalation improve long‑term survival?
What is the optimal duration of atezolizumab after surgery?

talk_title: Post‑neoadjuvant ctDNA predicts non‑pCR and distant recurrence in early triple‑negative breast cancer (NSABP B‑59/GEPRDUOS substudy)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA positivity post‑neoadjuvant
biomarkers.finding: 97.9% specificity for non‑pCR; HR 10.2 for distant recurrence (p=0.0001)

models.name: NSABP B‑59 / GEPRDUOS ctDNA substudy
models.genotype_or_key_features: 212 patients total; 155 with post‑neoadjuvant ctDNA data, early TNBC, randomized atezolizumab vs placebo

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for distant recurrence (ctDNA‑positive vs negative)

MOA_summary: Tumor‑informed ctDNA sequencing detects residual molecular disease after neoadjuvant therapy; persistent ctDNA strongly predicts lack of pathological complete response and a >10‑fold increased risk of distant recurrence in early triple‑negative breast cancer.
key_findings: Only 9% of patients remained ctDNA‑positive after neoadjuvant therapy.
ctDNA‑negative patients had an 85% pCR rate versus 2% in ctDNA‑positive patients.
Persistent ctDNA conferred a hazard ratio of 10.2 for distant recurrence (p=0.0001).
ctDNA clearance showed 97.9% specificity for predicting non‑pCR.
open_questions: Can ctDNA‑guided escalation (e.g., additional immunotherapy) improve outcomes for ctDNA‑positive patients?
What is the optimal timing for ctDNA assessment to inform adjuvant decisions?

cited_papers: Liu et al. 2024 J Clin Oncol

talk_title: Perioperative nivolumab: EFS and ctDNA dynamics in resectable NSCLC (CheckMate 770 exploratory analysis)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance (pre‑surgery)
biomarkers.finding: 75% clearance in nivolumab arm vs 46% in placebo; clearance associated with 70% PCR vs 18% in placebo

biomarkers.name: KEAP1 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Nivolumab improved EFS in KEAP1‑mutant patients (HR 0.6) compared with placebo

models.name: CheckMate 770 phase 3 perioperative nivolumab vs placebo
models.genotype_or_key_features: Stage II/III NSCLC, EGFR/ALK wild‑type, 41% biomarker evaluable (n≈190), median follow‑up 41 mo

clinical_data.metric: EFS hazard ratio in KEAP1‑mutant subgroup

MOA_summary: Nivolumab blocks PD‑1, reactivating anti‑tumor T‑cell responses; combined with chemotherapy in the peri‑operative setting it improves event‑free survival, especially in patients achieving ctDNA clearance and in tumors harboring KEAP1, SDK11, CDKN2A, or SMARCA4 alterations.
key_findings: Perioperative nivolumab improved EFS (HR 0.45) and increased 2‑year EFS to 74.8% versus 55.5% with placebo.
ctDNA clearance before surgery occurred in 75% of nivolumab patients versus 46% of placebo and correlated with higher PCR rates.
Nivolumab benefit persisted across genomic subgroups; patients with KEAP1, SDK11, CDKN2A, or SMARCA4 alterations derived greater relative benefit versus placebo.
Machine‑learning analysis identified ctDNA clearance, non‑N2 nodal status, PCR, squamous histology, and nivolumab treatment as top predictors of prolonged EFS.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with persistent ctDNA after neoadjuvant therapy had markedly lower PCR and higher MRD rates, indicating residual disease.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy may increase tumor antigen release, synergizing with PD‑1 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved EFS and pCR compared with chemo alone

open_questions: How should ctDNA‑positive, non‑clearing patients be managed post‑surgery?
Can integrating additional biomarkers (e.g., TMB) refine patient selection for peri‑operative nivolumab?

talk_title: Feasibility and efficacy of perioperative nivolumab ± relatlimab in potentially resectable HCC

speaker.name: Howard Lee
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University / Ohio State University

biomarkers.name: AFP dynamics
biomarkers.finding: Patients with decreasing AFP pre‑surgery had no recurrences to date

models.name: Phase II perioperative nivolumab ± relatlimab in HCC
models.genotype_or_key_features: 15 patients per arm, BCLC A‑C, diverse etiologies, median follow‑up 26.3 mo

MOA_summary: Nivolumab blocks PD‑1 while relatlimab blocks LAG‑3, together releasing T‑cell inhibition in the liver microenvironment; this dual checkpoint inhibition yields modest pathologic responses and favorable safety in resectable HCC.
key_findings: Both arms met feasibility criteria with no surgery‑delaying toxicities.
Pathologic or major response rates were 20% with nivolumab alone and 26.7% with the combination.
One‑year DFS was 73.3% (nivolumab) versus 100% (combo); two‑year OS favored the combination (84.8% vs 68.5%).
Grade 3+ immune‑related adverse events occurred in 46.7% (nivolumab) and 73.3% (combo), consistent with known safety profiles.
Decreasing AFP during neoadjuvant therapy correlated with absence of recurrence.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LAG‑3 receptor occupancy was lower in tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes than peripheral blood, suggesting limited tumor penetration at the tested dose.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade may overcome adaptive immune resistance mediated by FGL1‑LAG‑3 in the liver.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher pathologic response and DFS compared with nivolumab alone

open_questions: Would higher or intra‑arterial dosing of relatlimab improve tumor penetration and efficacy?
Can ctDNA or other molecular markers further stratify patients who benefit from dual checkpoint blockade?
